title: idmapped bind mounts can lose their mapping in certain configurations
body:
### Issue Description

IDMapped bind mounts lose mapping of their internals if the following is true:

- A volume was created, configured to bind to a location within an idmapped mount elsewhere
- The created volume is then mounted to an another container **without** idmapping.

The following script will reproduce the problematic setup and test it:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

image=registry.access.redhat.com/ubi10:latest
tmpdir=
common_flags=(
  --detach
  --rm
  --userns=auto
  --security-opt=label=disable
  --stop-signal=KILL
)
volume=
containers=()
cleanup() {
  if [[ ${#containers[@]} -gt 0 ]]; then
    echo "Stopping spawned containers"
    podman stop "${containers[@]}"
  fi
  if [[ -n "$volume" ]]; then
    echo "Removing created volumes"
    podman volume rm "$volume"
  fi
  if [[ -n "$tmpdir" ]]; then
    echo "Removing temporary directory"
    rm -rvf "$tmpdir"
  fi
}

trap cleanup EXIT

check() {
  local container=$1
  local target_uid=${2:-"$UID"}
  local check_uid
  check_uid=$(podman exec "$container" stat --format=%u /data/folder)
  if [[ $check_uid != "$target_uid" ]]; then
    echo -e "\e[1;31merror:\e[0m expected UID $target_uid but got $check_uid at /data/folder in $container"
    exit 1
  fi
}

run() {
  set -x
  "$@"
  set +x
}

set -euo pipefail

echo "Creating temporary directory"
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d /tmp/mnttest-XXXXXXXXXX)
mkdir -v "$tmpdir/folder"

echo "Creating test volume bind mounting $tmpdir/folder"
volume=$(run podman volume create --opt=type=bind --opt=device="$tmpdir/folder")
echo "Created volume: $volume"

mount_tmpdir=--mount=type=bind,source=$tmpdir,target=/data,ro,idmap
mount_volume=--mount=type=volume,source=$volume,target=/data/folder,ro

echo "Creating container bind mounting $tmpdir at /data with idmap"
containers+=("$(run podman run "${common_flags[@]}" "$mount_tmpdir" "$image" sleep inf)")
echo "Created container: ${containers[-1]}"

echo "Checking container ${containers[0]} view of /data/folder"
check "${containers[0]}"

echo "Creating container mounting volume $volume at /data, no idmap"
containers+=("$(run podman run "${common_flags[@]}" "$mount_volume" "$image" sleep inf)")
echo "Created container: ${containers[-1]}"

echo "Re-checking container ${containers[0]} view of /data/folder"
check "${containers[0]}"
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run the provided script as root

### Describe the results you received

```
Creating temporary directory

Creating test volume bind mounting /tmp/mnttest-gIHw9rvzgi/folder
```

### Podman in a container

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

### Additional information

This issue only showed up recently for me.

comments.body:
A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

why do you expect this to work when you don't specify `idmap` but still use a user namespace for the container?

If you specify `--userns=auto` without `idmap` for the volume, the files will be owned by the users in the user namespace (which are some random ranges since you are using `--userns=auto`).  These IDs are not mapped inside the first namespace so they appear as the overflow id 65534.  You either need to use `idmap` or don't use a user namespace for the second container.
if you want it to be not managed (and chowned), you can use a bind mount for the second container too.

> if you want it to be not managed (and chowned), you can use a bind mount for the second container too.

Is this documented anywhere? I could not find any reference in podman's documentation where mounting a volume causes the associated files to be chown-ed despite `chown`/`U` flags not being set. I was working under the assumption that podman would not `chown` anything out of the blue.

I intended to use volumes to make it easier to override specific mount points in quadlet configuration (by configuring the associated `.volume` source), but apparently volumes are more volatile than expected.

In any case, if chown was the intended behavior, then I agree that there aren't any issues here, barring missing(?) documentation.
opened a PR to document the behavior: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26500
